To optimize influence via the principle of consistency, we have to distinguish between two types of yes, specified and unspecified. Now a specified yes is of course what we want in response to our requests or recommendations or proposals because that form of ascent includes an active component that amplifies the pressure for later consistency with that yes. The unspecified version occurs when a person may have said yes internally but just hasn't actively indicated it yet. Relying on this form of yes may be wrong-headed for a pair of reasons. First, because people haven't said no to us, doesn't mean that they've said yes. They might be still deciding and we'd be foolish to end our persuasive efforts at such a crucial moment. Second, even if they have said yes to themselves, the passive form isn't nearly as potent as an active statement of ascent. This is why, after people have written down a decision, they become more willing to follow through on it even when they haven't shown the written decision to anyone. Just the step of committing themselves actively to a choice makes the choice more firm. Think of the Gordon's Restaurant example we described earlier. When the restaurant's receptionist said, please call if you have to cancel your reservation and stop there, she failed to get an active confirmation that customers would call. On the other hand, when she added the words, will you, to her request, and paused so people could confirm their intent, which they all did? The no-show rate dropped from 30% to 10%. So to amplify the impact of the consistency principle properly, you have to arrange for a specified yes from people. It doesn't matter if they say, write, or click yes. You just have to arrange for an active commitment, because that's where the influence is.
